name: Fionnuala the Wandering Fili
lines: The rift hums with half-remembered verses, wanderer. I have lost my meter chasing Bres's bridge — perhaps you'll finish the poem where I could not.
Every poet needs an ending. Mine keeps slipping down through the floors, always one verse further than I can follow.
I recite the old praise-songs to whatever still stands here. The stones don't answer, but they used to.
returnLine: You again. Good — I hadn't finished my verse about you yet.

name: The Cairn-Keeper
lines: I have watched over these stones since before the causeway split the dark. Every fallen thing bruises Bres's bridge a little more. Keep bruising it.
Each stone here was carried by someone who never came back for it. I've stopped counting. It stopped meaning anything, and started meaning everything.
The dead don't need keepers. I stay for the ones still walking — you, for instance, though you won't thank me for it.
returnLine: Still walking, I see. The cairn grows slower for it.

name: A Fomorian Deserter
lines: I want no more of Bres's bridge-building. Let it stay unfinished — for all our sakes, and yours.
They'll call me a coward for walking away. Let them. A finished bridge only leads somewhere worse.
I've seen what waits on the far side of Bres's causeway. You haven't. Turn back while turning back still means something.
returnLine: You're still descending? Stubborn. I respect it and I pity it in equal measure.

name: Aoife, Oath-Bound
introLine: Long ago I swore an oath the Otherworld itself couldn't unmake.

name: A Fomorian Tinker
introLine: Otherworld power for Otherworld power, wanderer. Hand me one of your Geasa, and I'll press something rarer into your palm.

name: The Seanchaí of the Mound
lines: Sit a while. Before Bres turned his back on Ériu, he was our king — a beautiful, miserly king who taxed the smoke of our fires. The land itself sickened under him.
Lugh came to Tara with every craft in his hands, and the doorkeeper laughed — 'we have one of each already.' 'But have you one who is all of them?' said Lugh. The door opened.
Three brothers forged for the gods: Goibniu the smith, Luchta the wright, Credne the brazier. No blade they made ever missed, and no wound it gave ever healed.
The Fomorians were not always monsters, you know. The sea remembers them as neighbours — hungry ones, but neighbours. It was Bres who taught them to take.
This mound is older than the war above it. The Sídhe dug deep when the light left — deeper than Bres will ever build.
They say the causeway he raises is made of everything Ériu lost — every stolen harvest, every cold hearth. Break it, and it all comes home.
At the second battle of Mag Tuired, it was not strength that won — it was every craft turned to one purpose. Remember that, when the stone falls faster.
When you meet him at last, look at his face. Bres means 'beautiful'. Ruin always is, from far enough away.
returnLine: Back from the dark, and still in one piece. Sit — the fire has kept your place.
